standards.title: CISA ZTMM Identity Pillar: Traditional Maturity
standards.description: At the traditional maturity level, identity management relies on static, on-premises identity solutions with password-based single-factor authentication. Identity is verified at the perimeter (VPN, firewall) and trusted implicitly within the network. Passwords are managed through manual processes, MFA is not broadly deployed, and identity stores are siloed across different applications and departments. There is limited visibility into authentication events, and access decisions are primarily based on network location rather than identity attributes. This represents the starting point from which organizations must evolve toward zero trust identity management.

standards.title: CISA ZTMM Identity Pillar: Initial Maturity
standards.description: At the initial maturity level, organizations begin implementing phishing-resistant MFA for privileged users and externally exposed applications. A centralized identity provider is deployed and integrated with critical applications. Basic identity governance processes are established including formal provisioning and deprovisioning workflows. Password policies are aligned with NIST SP 800-63B guidance (no composition rules, breach-checking). Initial user behavior analytics are deployed to detect anomalous authentication patterns. Self-service capabilities for password reset and MFA enrollment reduce helpdesk dependency while maintaining security. This level represents the first significant step toward identity-centric security.

standards.title: CISA ZTMM Identity Pillar: Advanced Maturity
standards.description: At the advanced maturity level, phishing-resistant MFA (FIDO2/WebAuthn) is deployed for all users, not just privileged ones. Continuous authentication and authorization replace one-time authentication events — the system continuously evaluates risk signals (device posture, location, behavior) and adjusts access accordingly. Automated identity lifecycle management is fully integrated with HR systems for joiners, movers, and leavers. Privileged access management (PAM) with just-in-time access and session recording is deployed for all administrative access. Identity federation is established with key partners and cloud services using standards like OIDC and SCIM. Cross-pillar integration connects identity signals with device, network, and data telemetry.

standards.title: CISA ZTMM Identity Pillar: Optimal Maturity
standards.description: At the optimal maturity level, passwordless authentication is the default for all users with hardware-backed cryptographic authenticators (FIDO2). Real-time risk-adaptive access control continuously adjusts permissions based on an aggregated trust score derived from identity, device, network, application, and data signals. AI/ML-driven user behavior analytics detect sophisticated threats including insider threats and advanced persistent threats. Fully automated identity governance with AI-assisted access certification ensures that access rights remain appropriate without manual review for low-risk changes. Decentralized identity technologies (verifiable credentials) are adopted for cross-organizational federation. The identity infrastructure is resilient with automated failover and self-healing capabilities.

standards.title: CISA ZTMM Device Pillar: Identity-Related Requirements
standards.description: The device pillar of the CISA Zero Trust Maturity Model requires that device identity be established and verified as part of access decisions. At initial maturity, a complete device inventory is maintained and devices are enrolled in a management platform. At advanced maturity, device compliance is checked in real-time as part of every access decision — non-compliant devices receive restricted or no access. Device certificates or hardware attestation (TPM-based) provide strong device identity that cannot be spoofed. At optimal maturity, device risk scores are continuously calculated from patch status, security agent health, encryption status, and behavioral signals, and these scores dynamically influence access decisions. Device identity binds with user identity to create a composite trust assessment.

standards.title: CISA ZTMM Cross-Cutting: Visibility and Analytics
standards.description: The visibility and analytics cross-cutting capability in the CISA Zero Trust Maturity Model requires comprehensive logging and analysis of all identity and access events across the enterprise. At traditional maturity, logging is basic and siloed. At initial maturity, identity logs are centralized in a SIEM with basic correlation rules. At advanced maturity, user and entity behavior analytics (UEBA) establish baselines and detect anomalies across authentication, authorization, and privilege usage. At optimal maturity, AI/ML-driven analytics correlate identity events with device, network, and data telemetry to detect sophisticated multi-stage attacks in near real-time. Visibility must span all identity providers, all applications, all network segments, and all data repositories to provide a complete picture of identity-related activity.

standards.title: CISA ZTMM Cross-Cutting: Automation and Orchestration
standards.description: The automation and orchestration cross-cutting capability requires that identity and access management processes are increasingly automated to reduce human error, improve response times, and scale with organizational growth. At initial maturity, basic provisioning and deprovisioning workflows are automated. At advanced maturity, automated responses to security events include dynamic access restriction, forced reauthentication, and automated account lockout based on SIEM/SOAR integration. At optimal maturity, self-healing identity infrastructure automatically remediates configuration drift, orchestrates cross-system access changes, and executes complex incident response playbooks without human intervention. Automation is essential for achieving zero trust at scale because manual processes cannot keep pace with the volume and velocity of access decisions required.

standards.title: CISA ZTMM Cross-Cutting: Governance
standards.description: The governance cross-cutting capability ensures that zero trust identity initiatives are aligned with organizational strategy, regulatory requirements, and risk management frameworks. Governance includes establishing a zero trust steering committee with executive sponsorship, defining identity-specific policies and standards, creating metrics and KPIs for measuring zero trust identity maturity progress (MFA adoption rate, passwordless percentage, access review completion rate, mean time to deprovision), conducting regular assessments against the maturity model, and maintaining a zero trust identity roadmap with clear milestones. Without strong governance, zero trust identity initiatives risk becoming fragmented technology deployments rather than a cohesive security transformation.

standards.title: All Data Sources and Computing Services Are Considered Resources
standards.description: In a zero trust architecture, all data sources and computing services are considered resources that require protection. This includes not just traditional servers and databases but also SaaS applications, serverless functions, IoT devices, user workstations, BYOD devices, and the identity infrastructure itself (directory services, certificate authorities, identity providers). Each resource SHALL have an identity and SHALL participate in the access control framework. The organization SHALL maintain a comprehensive inventory of all resources and SHALL define access policies for each. This tenet eliminates the concept of 'trusted' internal resources that require no authentication — every resource, regardless of location, requires authenticated and authorized access.

standards.title: All Communication Is Secured Regardless of Network Location
standards.description: Zero trust requires that all communication be encrypted and authenticated regardless of whether it occurs within or outside the traditional network perimeter. Internal network traffic SHALL be treated with the same level of suspicion as external traffic. Mutual TLS (mTLS) SHALL be implemented for service-to-service communication. User-to-service communication SHALL be encrypted using TLS 1.2 or higher. Network location (e.g., being on the corporate LAN) SHALL NOT automatically grant trust or reduce authentication requirements. This tenet directly challenges the traditional castle-and-moat security model where internal network traffic is implicitly trusted, and is one of the most fundamental shifts in zero trust thinking.

standards.title: Access to Individual Enterprise Resources Is Granted on a Per-Session Basis
standards.description: Access to resources SHALL be granted on a per-session basis with the minimum privileges needed for the task. Trust in the requester SHALL be evaluated before access is granted, and this evaluation SHALL be based on the current state of the requester's identity, device, behavior, and environment — not on historical trust or previous sessions. Access tokens SHALL have limited lifetimes and SHALL be scoped to specific resources and operations. Just-in-time (JIT) access provisioning SHALL be preferred over standing privileges, especially for administrative access. This tenet means that authentication to one resource does not grant implicit access to other resources, and that access decisions are continuously reevaluated throughout the session.

standards.title: Access Is Determined by Dynamic Policy
standards.description: Access to resources is determined by dynamic policy — including the observable state of client identity, application or service, and the requesting asset — and may include other behavioral and environmental attributes. The policy engine SHALL consider: the identity of the requester (user or service), the state of the authentication (AAL, freshness), the device posture (compliance, patch level, encryption status), the requested resource and operation, the time of request, and the current threat level. Policies SHALL be defined declaratively and enforced consistently across all access points. Dynamic policy enables the organization to respond to changing conditions (new threats, device compromise, behavioral anomalies) by adjusting access in real-time without requiring manual intervention.

standards.title: Enterprise Monitors and Measures Integrity and Security Posture of All Assets
standards.description: The enterprise SHALL continuously monitor and measure the integrity and security posture of all owned and associated assets. No asset is inherently trusted — trust must be earned through continuous compliance verification. The monitoring system SHALL evaluate device health (OS patch level, antivirus status, endpoint detection and response agent health, disk encryption status, screen lock configuration), application integrity (code signing verification, version currency), and identity system health (identity provider availability, certificate validity, federation trust status). Assets that fail to meet minimum security requirements SHALL have their access reduced or revoked until remediation is verified. This continuous assessment replaces the traditional binary trust model with a graduated trust score.

standards.title: All Resource Authentication and Authorization Are Dynamic and Strictly Enforced
standards.description: All resource authentication and authorization are dynamic and strictly enforced before access is allowed. This is an iterative cycle of scanning, assessing threats, adapting, and continuously authenticating and authorizing. The organization SHALL implement continuous authentication that reevaluates the access decision throughout the session, not just at the initial authentication event. If risk signals change during a session (device becomes non-compliant, user behavior becomes anomalous, threat level increases), the system SHALL be able to step up authentication requirements, restrict access, or terminate the session. This requires tight integration between the identity provider, the policy engine, security monitoring systems, and the application or resource being accessed.

standards.title: Enterprise Collects Maximum Possible Data and Uses It to Improve Security Posture
standards.description: The enterprise SHALL collect as much information as possible about the current state of assets, network infrastructure, and communications and use it to improve its security posture. Data collected SHALL include authentication events, authorization decisions, network flows, endpoint telemetry, application logs, and user behavior patterns. This data SHALL be analyzed to identify trends, detect threats, measure the effectiveness of security controls, and inform policy adjustments. Machine learning and advanced analytics SHALL be applied to the collected data to identify patterns that are not apparent through rule-based analysis. The collected data SHALL also be used to refine and improve the access policies over time, creating a feedback loop where security posture continuously improves based on observed behavior and outcomes.

standards.title: Policy Decision Point and Policy Enforcement Point Architecture
standards.description: The NIST SP 800-207 zero trust architecture defines two critical logical components: the Policy Decision Point (PDP) and the Policy Enforcement Point (PEP). The PDP consists of the Policy Engine (PE) which makes the access decision, and the Policy Administrator (PA) which establishes and terminates the communication path between the subject and the resource. The PEP is the system component that enables, monitors, and eventually terminates the connection between the subject and the resource based on directives from the PA. All access requests flow through the PEP to the PDP, where a trust algorithm evaluates identity, device, resource, environmental, and behavioral attributes against defined policies. This architecture ensures that every access request is evaluated independently and that policy enforcement is consistent across all access paths.

standards.title: Zero Trust Trust Algorithm
standards.description: The trust algorithm is the process used by the Policy Engine to grant or deny access to a resource. Input to the trust algorithm includes: the access request itself (requester identity, requested resource, requested action), the subject database (role, historical behavior, assigned attributes), the asset database (device posture, OS version, installed software, credentials), the resource requirements (sensitivity level, required AAL, required device posture), and threat intelligence (current threat level, known IOCs, recent attack patterns). The trust algorithm combines these inputs — either through a scoring system (assigning numerical confidence values to each input) or a contextual analysis (evaluating each input against a set of qualitative criteria) — to produce an access decision. The algorithm should favor denying access when confidence is low to minimize risk.

standards.title: Zero Trust Deployment: Enhanced Identity Governance (SDP)
standards.description: The enhanced identity governance deployment model for zero trust uses the identity of actors as the key component of policy creation. Resource access policies are based on identity and assigned attributes. The primary requirement for resource access is the identity of the requesting actor and their assigned privileges. Enterprise resource access is gated through a policy enforcement point managed by the policy administrator. This model works well when organizations have a robust identity governance program and a well-defined RBAC or ABAC model. It is often implemented using Software Defined Perimeter (SDP) or identity-aware proxy patterns where all access is brokered through an identity-aware gateway that evaluates the requester's identity attributes against the resource's access policy before granting access.

standards.title: Zero Trust Deployment: Micro-Segmentation
standards.description: The micro-segmentation deployment model for zero trust places individual or small groups of resources on their own network segment protected by a gateway security device that acts as the PEP. The gateway controls access by using identity-aware policies — to reach a resource, the requester must first authenticate and be authorized by the gateway. Micro-segmentation can be implemented using next-generation firewalls, software-defined networking, or host-based segmentation agents. In the identity context, micro-segmentation ensures that network access alone is insufficient — identity verification is required at each segment boundary. This approach is particularly effective for protecting high-value assets such as identity infrastructure (domain controllers, certificate authorities, identity providers) from lateral movement attacks.

standards.title: CISA ZTMM: MFA Maturity Progression
standards.description: The CISA Zero Trust Maturity Model defines a clear progression for multi-factor authentication deployment. At the traditional level, MFA may not be deployed or is limited to VPN access. At the initial level, MFA is deployed for all externally-exposed applications and for privileged users, primarily using app-based push notifications or TOTP. At the advanced level, phishing-resistant MFA (FIDO2/WebAuthn) is deployed for all users with hardware security keys for privileged users. At the optimal level, passwordless authentication using FIDO2 passkeys is the default, with risk-adaptive step-up authentication triggered by anomalous conditions. Each maturity level represents a significant reduction in identity-based attack risk, with the transition from basic MFA to phishing-resistant MFA being particularly impactful given the rise of adversary-in-the-middle (AiTM) phishing attacks.

standards.title: CISA ZTMM: Identity Lifecycle Automation Maturity
standards.description: Identity lifecycle automation progresses through the CISA ZTMM maturity levels. At the traditional level, identity provisioning and deprovisioning are manual processes driven by helpdesk tickets. At the initial level, automated workflows handle basic provisioning triggered by HR events, but modifications and deprovisioning may still require manual intervention. At the advanced level, full lifecycle automation integrates HR systems (Workday, SAP) with the identity provider and downstream applications using SCIM protocol, ensuring that identity changes propagate within minutes rather than days. Role-based access is automatically assigned based on HR attributes (department, title, location). At the optimal level, AI-driven access recommendations suggest appropriate access based on peer analysis, and continuous access certification automatically removes unused entitlements.

standards.title: CISA ZTMM: Privileged Access Management Maturity
standards.description: Privileged access management maturity in the CISA ZTMM progresses from shared admin credentials at the traditional level to fully automated just-in-time access at the optimal level. At initial maturity, dedicated admin accounts are established and basic credential vaulting is deployed. At advanced maturity, just-in-time (JIT) privileged access is implemented — standing privileges are eliminated and administrative access is provisioned on-demand for a limited duration with full session recording. Privileged Access Workstations (PAWs) or jump servers are required for all administrative tasks. At optimal maturity, AI-driven anomaly detection monitors all privileged sessions in real-time, automated workflow approval gates enforce separation of duties for high-risk operations, and zero standing privilege is the default for all administrative roles.

standards.title: CISA ZTMM Network Pillar: Identity-Aware Networking
standards.description: The network pillar of the CISA Zero Trust Maturity Model requires that network access decisions incorporate identity signals. At initial maturity, network segmentation separates identity infrastructure from general-purpose networks. At advanced maturity, identity-aware network access control (NAC) using 802.1X requires authentication before granting network access. Software-defined perimeter (SDP) or identity-aware proxy (IAP) solutions route all application traffic through an identity verification layer, eliminating direct network access to resources. At optimal maturity, micro-segmentation with identity-aware policies ensures that network access is continuously validated against the requester's identity, device posture, and behavioral context. Network-level encryption (IPsec, WireGuard) provides mutual authentication between endpoints.
